spans.content: Surface thermal stability in extremely fine-grained metals: Surface grain
boundary reconstruction and its initiating destabilization
Jinguo Lin a,b , Feng Liu
\mathrm { a , b , } ^ { \ast } \mathrm { \Phi } _ { \mathrm { c } }
, Wenqiang Cheng c , Yan Ma
a,b

a State Key Laboratory of Nonlinear Mechanics, Institute of Mechanics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Schwarz crystals
Surface thermal stability
Quadruple junction lines
Surface energy dependence
Detwinning
Band-shaped destabilization
A B S T R A C T
The Schwarz crystal embodies a metastable polycrystalline structure distinguished by its extremely fine grains
< 1 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }
in size), stabilized via curved grain boundaries (GBs) that are geometrically constrained by coherent
twin boundaries (CTBs). This unique configuration bestows upon it exceptional thermal stability remaining
stable even as temperatures near the melting point, and synergistically achieving unparalleled strength. Capi-
talizing on these remarkable attributes, the most promising application in the near future lies in surface
Schwarzation, a technique poised to elevate the properties of metal crystal surfaces. However, a major challenge
in this respect arises from the surface thermal stability of Schwarz crystals: Unlike their counterparts in the bulk,
which possess well-defined curved GBs and thus lack a driving force for GB migration, the continuity of GBs at
the surface is disrupted, leading to uncertainty in their thermal stability. Through atomistic simulations, we
demonstrate the high surface thermal stability of Schwarz crystals, and reveal both surface GB reconstruction and
its initiating destabilization. This study not only enhances our fundamental understanding of surface thermal
stability in Schwarz crystals, but also establishs the theoretical basis for the application of surface Schwarzation.
1. Introduction
Despite nanocrystalline (NC) metals possess exceptional properties
(e.g., high strength), the inherent poor thermal stability has constrained
their practical applications, thereby highlighting the trade-off between
strength and thermal stability [1,2]. Consequently, a paradigm shift has
occurred in NC metal research over the past decade, with significant
efforts being directed toward enhancing thermal stability [1].
Previous researches demonstrate that conventional approaches to
achieving thermal stability in NC metals can be classified into two cat-
egories: thermodynamic stabilization and kinetic stabilization. For
instance, solute segregation in grain boundaries (GBs) is a strategy of
thermodynamic stabilization by reducing the driving force for grain
growth, which has been proven to be successful in overcoming the
intrinsic poor thermal stability of NC metals [3,4]. In contrast to ther-
modynamic stabilization, the kinetic stabilization of NC materials
operates through suppression of GB mobility, which can be achieved via
diverse kinetic mechanisms, including solute drag induced by solute
atoms [5] and Zener pinning caused by nanoscale particles [6].
In 2018, researchers discovered an anomalous size effect on thermal
stability in NC Cu and Ni: when grain size is reduced below ~70 nm (Cu)
or
{ \sim } 5 0 \mathrm { n m }
(Ni), the coarsening temperature unexpectedly increases due
to GBs autonomously relax into low-energy states via interactions with
partial dislocations [7]. This GB relaxation reduced excess energy,
enhancing thermal stability of NC metals [7,8]. The finding sheds light
on stabilizing NC structures in metals.
Along this path, subsequently, Lu’s group has prepared extremely
fine grains pushing thermal stability toward its theoretical limits. This
extremely fine grain is also known as Schwarz crystal, which in-
corporates curved GBs and coherent twin boundaries (CTBs). Schwarz
crystals constitute a paradigm-shifting class of metastable poly-
crystalline structures, characterized by extremely fine grain sizes
\mathord { \left. \kern - delimiterspace \right.} < 1 0
nm) and exceptional thermal stability alongside superior mechanical
properties [9,10].
The atomistic model of the Schwarz crystal is shown in Fig. 1(a),
which consists of curved GBs and CTBs network (see the right of Fig. 1

(a)). With the minimal interface structure, i.e. curved GBs, possessing
zero mean curvature, the driving force for GB migration tends to vanish,
as it is proportional to the GB curvature according to the equation, i.e., v
\mathbf { \Psi } = M _ { 0 } \mathbf { e x p } \ : ( \mathbf { \Psi } - \mathbf { \Psi } H _ { a } / k _ { B } T ) \boldsymbol { \kappa } \gamma ,
, where
, kBT, κ, and γ are the GB
mobility, the activation energy, the thermal energy, mean curvature of
GB, and GB energy, respectively [11]. It should be noted that the curved
GBs are further constrained by high-density CTBs on both sides (Fig. 1
(b)), allowing them to remain stable even at temperatures near the
melting point and exhibit exceptional high strength [12].
Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that surface Schwarzation, i.e.
generation of a surface layer of Schwarz crystal (the related illustrations
of surface Schwarzation could be found in Supplementary Note 1 and
Fig. S1) [11], provides a viable means to substantially elevate the sur-
face properties and markedly enhance the overall performance, which
could be particularly effective in bolstering strength [9,10], enhancing
thermal stability [10,13–15], and consolidating the alloy phase by
suppressing atomic diffusion [16,17]. These remarkable attributes po-
sition surface Schwarzation as a versatile solution with extensive po-
tential applications across various sectors, including aerospace,
machinery manufacturing, and healthcare. Specifically, it can be uti-
lized in high-temperature materials [9], cutting blades [18], and
corrosion-resistant implant coatings [19], thereby underscoring its
broad and significant impact.
Despite the ultrastability of Schwarz crystals, surface Schwarzation
encounters numerous challenges due to the prevalence of material
failures occurring at surfaces [20–22], such as fatigue fracture [23],
fretting fatigue [24], wear [25], and corrosion [26], etc. These failures
are strongly related to the microstructures and their evolutions at sur-
faces under external fields. In contrast to its bulk phase, the surface of a
material disrupts the structural continuity, necessitating adjustments in
the surface microstructure to minimize surface energy. This phenome-
non is well-documented in various contexts of instability of micro-
structure, such as the surface energy-driven abnormal growth of grain
sizes in thin films [27–30] and surface reconstruction events [31–35].
Surface Schwarzation is anticipated to confer greatly enhanced surface
properties. Nevertheless, exposure to relatively high temperatures can
induce above-mentioned grain growth and surface reconstruction,
resulting in a reduction in surface hardness and the loss of other notable
mechanical and physical properties [1,36–39]. Therefore, the greatest
challenge in surface Schwarzation is surface thermal stability, which
essentially differs from bulk and ultimately determines the range of
temperatures within which it can be effectively applied.
It holds fundamental significance to study thermal stability and un-
cover corresponding underlying mechanisms for surface Schwarzation.
Unlike in the bulk, where GBs lack a driving force for GB migration in
perfect Schwarz crystals, the surfaces of Schwarz crystals experience
disruptions of periodicity and continuity of curved GBs. It raises ques-
tions about the surface thermal stability of the Schwarz crystals, which is
directly related to the feasibility of surface Schwarzation.
The primary focus of this paper is to explore the surface thermal
stability of Schwarz crystals in depth, which is the foundation for surface
Schwarzation application. Through atomistic simulations, we study the
influence of surfaces on the thermal stability. The unique surface GB
reconstruction and destabilization behaviors, along with the resultant
microstructural features, have been observed, and the corresponding
mechanisms have been elucidated. Our findings will not only contribute
to the fundamental understanding of surface thermal stability in
Schwarz crystals, but also pioneer new avenues for the application of
surface Schwarzation.
2. Materials and methods
Schwarz crystals feature a network architecture comprising CTBs
and curved GBs. These curved GBs form a Schwarz D-minimal interface,
which is one of the simplest triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) [9].
In differential geometry, TPMS is a minimal surface in
three-dimensional Euclidean space, characterized by exhibiting zero
mean curvature at all points. The first examples of TPMS were described
by Hermann Schwarz in 1865, followed by the discovery of various
additional TPMS structures in subsequent studies [40,41]. TPMS exist
widely and have been observed in various systems, such as biological
membranes [42], block copolymers [43], and equipotential surfaces in
crystals [44], among others. Physically, zero mean curvature eliminates
the thermodynamic driving force for GB migration [10]. Moreover, the
stable CTBs networks and Schwarz D-minimal interface mutually
interlock, further preventing GBs from migration [9,12]. Some general
contexts for the stability of Schwarz crystals are provided in the Sup-
plementary Note 2.
The Schwarz crystals now have been discovered in various elemental
metals, such as Cu [9,10], Al [14], and Pt [13,15]. Schwarz crystal is
first observed in elemental Cu with its average grain size around 10 nm
[9,10]. Subsequently, it is also observed in Al [14] and Pt [13,15]. These
materials all possess ultrahigh thermal and mechanical stability. The
GBs of these materials exhibit curved interface characteristics, which
b

2 \times 2 \times 2
supercell, where pink and white represent coherent twin
boundaries (CTBs) and grain boundaries (GBs), respectively (Left). It should be noted that to enhance the visualization of GB structures, bulk atoms are excluded.
Curved GBs (Top right), i.e., minimal interface structure, and CTB network (Bottom right) are shown. (b) A cross-sectional view of the Schwarz crystal reveals that
curved GBs are constrained by CTB networks through quadruple junctions, which are marked by red circles.
J. Lin et al.
Acta Materialia 303 (2026) 121730
share common features with the minimal interface structures obtained
through computer simulations (Fig. S2). In particular, typical honey-
combed grains with circular shapes are observed, and they demonstrate
a symmetry similar to that of the Schwarz D-surface.
Schwarz crystals are also found in alloy systems [16,17]. Specifically,
in Al-Mg alloys, atomic diffusion is effectively suppressed, which pre-
vents intermetallic precipitation from nanosized grains and their
coarsening up to the equilibrium melting temperature [16]. Similar in
Al-Zn alloys, spinodal decomposition within supersaturated Al(Zn) solid
solutions is found to be suppressed [17]. Formation of Schwarz crystal
provides a universal strategy to stabilize supersaturated phases, thereby
enabling broadened compositional windows for property modulation in
alloys.
Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are carried out to examine the
thermal stability of Schwarz crystals with or without surfaces. For the
generations of Schwarz crystals, the method proposed in a published
literature has been applied [9,10,45]. Firstly, the Kelvin crystal model
comprises 16 truncated octahedron (Kelvin cells) of uniform size,
fundamentally resembling a body-centered cubic structure extending up
to the second-nearest neighbor. Here, the length of the Kelvin cells is set
to
3 . 1 5 \ \mathrm { n m }
, which is reasonable size based on the experimental results.
Secondly, Kelvin crystal model is heated to approach a target tempera-
ture at a constant heating rate of
8 ~ \mathrm { K } / \mathrm { n s }
, and the NPH ensemble is
employed in this process (detailed illustration could be seen in Supple-
mentary Note 3 and Fig. S3).To evaluate the thermal stability, thermal
relaxation simulations are performed at different temperatures (tem-
perature interval is 50 K), where the NVT ensemble is employed in this
process. In both the heating and relaxation procedures, the time step is
set to 2.5 fs and the interatomic potential is described by the
semi-empirical embedded atom method (EAM) many-body potential for
Schwarz crystal models with surfaces are obtained by inserting a
certain thickness of vacuum layer in the supercells (i.e.
2 \times 2 \times 2
3 { \times } 3 { \times } 3
and
2 \times 2 \times 6 )
. The orientation of a surface is defined according to the
primitive cell of the Schwarz crystal model. For example, the normal
direction of the (001) surface aligns with the Z-axis of the Schwarz
crystal model. It is important to note that all surface orientations are
systematically named following this crystallographic convention.
In addition, the surface energy, i.e.
\gamma = ( E _ { s } - E _ { b } ) / A
, can be calcu-
lated. Here
E _ { s }
and
E _ { b }
are the potential energies of Schwarz crystal with
surfaces and without surfaces, respectively, and A represents the surface
area. It is important to note that all configurations undergo a minimi-
zation process prior to energy calculation. Given that temperature ef-
fects are not incorporated into the minimization procedure, surface
reconstruction does not occur.
All of the simulations performed in this study utilize the LAMMPS
package [47], with periodic boundary conditions applied across all three
dimensions. Visualization of atomic configurations is achieved through
the OVITO software [48]. Specifically, dislocation extraction algorithm
(DXA) [49] enables differentiation of CTBs and curved GBs.
Here, we also have summarized some hypotheses in the study as
follows:
1. The central hypothesis is that the perfect Schwarz crystal without
defects (except for surface) is a representative configuration for study
thermal stability. The GBs in the NC structure act as efficient defect
sinks, minimizing defects introduced during sample preparation. As
a result, the impact of residual defects on thermal stability could be
very limited.
2. Besides, the influence of surface morphology on thermal stability
could be negligible. This is because of the fact that the characteristic
lengths of surface roughness and structural inhomogeneity are
significantly larger than the grain size of the Schwarz crystals.
Consequently, the thermal stability of Schwarz crystals is predomi-
nantly governed by their intrinsic structural features rather than
surface morphology.
Moreover, our simulation protocols are assumed to be well-suited for
investigating the thermal stability of Schwarz crystals. Given that the
Schwarz crystal is merely a few nanometers in size, the duration and
spatial scale of our present simulations have substantially surpassed the
characteristic time and length scales of the Schwarz crystal. More
importantly, the long-time atomic diffusion is effectively suppressed
according to experiments [16], thereby the duration of current simula-
tions is enough to capture main physical processes related to thermal
stability.
3. Results and discussion
3.1. The high surface thermal stability of Schwarz crystals
Experimental studies and MD simulations have demonstrated the
extraordinary thermal stability and ultrahigh mechanical strength of
Schwarz crystals [9,10]. However, it is important to note that existing
research has exclusively examined idealized systems without involving
surface influence. To evaluate surface impacts on the thermal stability,
two atomic models
( 2 \times 2 \times 2
supercells) are constructed: one with
representative (001) surfaces and one without, as shown in Fig. 2(a).
Both models feature Schwarz crystals with a grain size of 4.45 nm. The
blue, purple, red, and grey atoms represent face-centered cubic (FCC),
body-centered cubic (BCC), hexagonal close-packed (HCP) (which are
identified as CTBs), and other atoms, respectively.
After thermal equilibration at
1 0 0 0 \mathrm { ~ K ~ }
(see details in the Methods
section), structural evolutions are analyzed. Fig. 2(b) reveals negligible
changes in the surface-free model (consistent with prior studies that
perfect Schwarz crystals could maintain stability near the melting point,
i.e., 1353 K [46]), whereas the surface-containing model exhibits sig-
nificant structural evolution, where curved GBs and CTBs vanish over
time. Notably, at a slightly lower temperature of
8 0 0 ~ \mathrm { K } ,
, both of these
two systems could maintain their structural integrity. More quantitative
analyses show negligible energy fluctuations post-relaxation at 800K,
while at 1000 K the surface-containing system undergoes abrupt energy
reduction (Fig. 2(c) and Fig. S5) and atomic packing undergoes a sudden
change around
\scriptstyle \mathrm { t } = 2 5 0 0
ps (Fig. 2(d)), driven by single-crystallization
marked by increasing FCC atoms and declining HCP and other atoms.
Eventually, all curved GBs and CTBs vanish, and the system transforms
to a single crystal state. Besides, it is meaningful to construct a larger
scale model (Fig. S6). The detailed illustrations could be found in Sup-
plementary Note 4.
These results clearly indicate that Schwarz crystals maintain their
structural integrity at relatively high temperatures, even when surfaces
are involved. In the subsequent sections, we will systematically explore
the surface microstructure evolution characteristics and delve into the
underlying mechanisms.
3.2. Surface GB reconstruction: surface quadruple junction lines (QLs)
formation
In this section, we propose to uncover its surface structural feature,
which may provide clues for its ultrahigh surface thermal stability.
Given that the GBs within a Schwarz crystal possess curvatures that
approach zero, the migration velocity (v) of GBs is close to zero based on
the equation, i.e.,
\nu = M _ { 0 } \mathrm { e x p } \big ( - H _ { a } / k _ { B } T \big ) \kappa \gamma ,
, where
, Ha, kBT, κ and γ
are the GB mobility, the activation energy, the thermal energy, mean
curvature of GB, and GB energy, respectively [11]. However, due to the
discontinuity of the minimal interface structure at the surface, the mean
curvature cannot be well defined at this location (Fig. S7). Physically,
the interfacial tension equilibrium of GBs at surfaces cannot reach.
Therefore, surface GB reconstruction should be performed at elevated
temperatures to reduce surface energy.
To capture this process, the GB structure evolutions of representative
(001) surfaces are monitored at 800 K which is high enough for surface
GB reconstruction but lower than its instability temperature, and shown

in Fig. 3. As can be seen, most of the internal structure of the system
remains preserved, only surface GB reconstruction takes place (the left
column of Fig. 3(a)). To carefully examine the specific atomic structure
near the surfaces, partially zoomed in views of the upper and lower
surfaces are presented in the right column of Fig. 3(a). It is evident that
the surfaces of Schwarz crystals can stably exist in the form of quadruple
junction lines (QLs) formed by CTBs with deformed surfaces. Notably,
QLs are found for the aggregation growth and evolution processes of
penta-twinned gold nanoparticles [50].
To elucidate the formation processes of QLs on the (001) surfaces,
Fig. 3(b) shows three atomic structures’ snapshots at different time
points. For instance, once the initial QLs are destructed, which are
formed by the CTBs and curved GBs near the top surface, new CTBs
emerge from the top surface at
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t } = 2 5 0
ps. These CTBs eventually link up
with the nearby curved boundaries, leading to the formation of QLs at
the surfaces at
\scriptstyle \mathrm { t = } 1 3 7 5
ps. It is worth noting that QLs formation only
appears in the vicinity of the surface, other QLs remain well-preserved
within the bulk.
To validate our simulation results, we have successfully experimen-
tally synthesized polycrystalline pure Cu samples with extremely fine
grains via a cryogenic plastic deformation technique referring to a
previously validated protocol [9,13,14,16,17], with subsequent thermal
stability measurements and preliminary microstructure characterization
using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Our tests have uncov-
ered high thermal stability and characteristic microstructures, i.e. grains
with average size of
1 0 \mathrm { n m }
including QL structure resemble the features
predicted by our simulation models (Fig. S8 and Supplementary Note 5).
These results emphasize the pivotal role played by QLs in surface
thermal stability, which will be further stuided in following sections.
3.3. Surface-initiated destabilization of Schwarz crystals
Although the surface GB reconstruction could considerably stabilize
the microstructures at the surface, eventually an early destabilization
relative to its bulk phase should be initiated from the surface. In this
section, we examine the characteristics of structural evolution during
the destabilization process.
To proceed, the atomic model
2 \times 2 \times 6
supercell) with representative
(001) surfaces is applied, as illustrated in the Fig. 4(a). Additionally, at
1000 K the evolutions of potential energy and the fractions of atomic
packing are shown in Fig. 4(b) and Fig. 4(c), respectively. It is evident
that at
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t = } 4 7 5
ps, there is a notable change in both energy and atomic
packing, resembling the changes observed in the smaller system (Fig. 2
(c-d)). From this time point, the CTBs and curved GBs within the system

gradually disappear, triggering the single crystallization transformation.
The specific symmetric destabilization process is snapshotted and shown
in Fig. 4(d) and Fig. S9. At
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t = } 1 2 5
ps, the atoms around the top and
bottom boundaries have undergon surface melting [51–53] (highlighted
by the grey atoms on the surfaces). As time progresses, the disorderly
arranged atoms at surfaces undergoe a gradual transition to a nearly
single-crystal state
( \mathbf { t } { = } 2 5 0 ~ \mathbf { p s } )
. As time increases further to 375 ps, two
interfaces between the Schwarz crystal and single crystals emerge within
the system, indicated by the black dash lines. Subsequently, these upper
and lower interfaces gradually move towards the interior until they ul-
timately meet (as indicated by the black arrows), marking the comple-
tion of single crystallization (at
\scriptstyle \mathrm { t } = 5 0 0
ps). It should be noted that, the
evolution of potential energy is correlated with surface structural
transformations in Schwarz crystals with surface (Supplementary Note 6
and Fig. S10).
In this case, the destabilization in Schwarz crystals symmetrically
initiates from surface layers on both sides and progressively propagates
toward the interior regions.
3.4. Surface energy dependent thermal stability and its correlation with
junction lines’ formation
Previous results suggest that QLs structure formed through high-
temperature surface GB reconstruction can stabilize surfaces and
inhibit thermal degradation. To investigate the relationship between
QLs and thermal stability, and to elucidate the critical physical param-
eters governing thermal stability, numerous thermal relaxation simula-
tions across varying temperatures are performed.
As a representative case, there is apparent discrepancy between the
top and bottom (001) surfaces when the height is equal to
\mathbf { \dot { 3 5 0 } \mathring { A } }
(far left
of Fig. 5(a)), as a result, the asymmetrical thermal destabilization mode
at
1 0 0 0 \mathrm { K }
is shown in Fig. 5(a) and Fig. S11, where red and grey atoms
represent HCP and other atoms, respectively. When
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t = } 6 2 . 5
ps, the re-
gions surrounding the top and bottom boundaries firstly transform to

\scriptstyle { \mathrm { t } } = 3 7 5
ps, the vicinity of the top
boundaries transforms to FCC single crystal (marked by white back-
ground), while the bottom region is stable due to the fact that the bottom
surface has undergone GB reconstruction to form the QLs. As time
further increases, the interface between the Schwarz crystal and FCC
single crystal gradually moves toward the bottom surface during
relaxation, ultimately leading to the near-complete transformation of

ps. This surface-sensitive
instability behavior highlights surface dependence of thermal stability
and the critical role of QLs against thermal destabilization.
This asymmetrical phenomenon leads to interesting questions: Is
there any structural difference in the impact of various surfaces on the
thermal stability? Does the critical instability temperature
exhibit
an inherent correlation with surface energy (γ)?
To address these questions, several calculation models with different
surfaces are established (Fig. S12). The schematic model is shown in the
inset of Fig. 5(b), where
\mathrm { h _ { i } }
and
\mathtt { h _ { c } }
represent the initial and current height
of the model, respectively, red and grey lines represent CTBs and curved
GBs, respectively. In all simulations, the
\mathrm { h _ { c } }
is always smaller than
\mathrm { h _ { i } }
(i.e.
\mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { i } = 3 7 9 } \mathrm { \bar { A } } )
and the corresponding height difference is achieved through
selective removal of green-rectangle-marked atomic layers. It can be
seen that there are obvious differences in microstructures near the upper
surfaces for different models (Fig. S12), as a result, the surface energies
(for the as-prepared surface) of these models are significantly different
as shown in Fig 5(b). Besides, the corresponding functions of potential
energy as temperature are shown in Fig. S13, where the critical change-
points of potential energies correspond to the destabilization. It can be
seen that there are enormous differences in the critical instability tem-
peratures of these models marked by blue hollow circles in Fig. 5(c).
Fig. 5(c) also illustrates the correlation between the calculated critical
instability temperatures and surface energies, revealing that a higher
surface energy corresponds to a lower critical instability temperature.
This surface-energy-governed destabilization mechanism aligns with
established principles of surface-mediated thermal degradation, where
interfacial energy states dictate system stability limits [51].
To understand this correlation, a theoretical model is employed to
compare with our calculated results (details can be seen in Fig. S14 and
Supplementary Note 7 [54]). The phenomenon of surface melting
observed in surface destabilization of Schwarz crystals can be described
quantitatively. The total change in free energy (per unit area) can be
written as [54]
\Delta G = d \rho \lambda \left(1 - \frac {T}{T _ {m}}\right) + \left(\sigma_ {S D} + \sigma_ {D V} - \sigma_ {S V}\right) + V (d) \tag {1}
where
d
is the thickness of disorder layer, λ is the enthalpy of fusion per
unit mass,
T _ { m }
is the melting temperature, and
\rho
is the disorder layer
density.
\sigma _ { S D }
, σDV, and
\sigma _ { S V }
are the free energies of Schwarz crystal-
disorder, disorder-vacuum, and Schwarz crystal-vacuum interfaces,
respectively (Fig. S14). The term
V ( d )
represents effective repulsive
interaction between the Schwarz crystal-disorder and disorder-vacuum
interfaces. It can be described as
V ( d ) = \alpha \mathbf { e x p } \left( - 2 d / \xi \right)
for small
d
where
\alpha
and
\xi
are a parameter and correlation length in the disorder
layer, respectively.
Based on the principle that the total energy of the system tends to
minimize, i.e.
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \mathrm { d } \Delta G } { \mathrm { d } d } = 0 } \end{array}
, the critical instability temperature
T _ { c }
can be
derived as
\begin{array} { r } { T _ { c } = T _ { m } \bigg ( 1 + \frac { 2 \Delta \sigma } { \rho \lambda \xi } \bigg ) } \end{array}
, where
( \Delta \sigma = \sigma _ { S D } + \sigma _ { D V } - \sigma _ { S V } )
. As the
\sigma _ { S D }
and
\sigma _ { D V }
are constants, and
\sigma _ { S V }
is effectively equivalent to the surface
energy of Schwarz, then the relationship
( T _ { c } \propto - \gamma )
can be derive, where γ
is the surface energy of Schwarz crystal.
According to the Fig. 5(c), the theoretical results (red line) above are
in good agreement with the simulations results (blue hollow circles),
thus surface energy indeed plays an important role in surface thermal
stability for Schwarz crystals. It should be noted that, surface GB re-
constructions have already finished at the critical instability tempera-
ture of these samples, resulting in the formation of junction lines
(Fig. S15), which are beneficial for increasing thermal stability. For
instance, two representative structures, i.e. QLs formed by CTBs and
surface atoms
( \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } } ^ { \mathrm { - } } 3 6 0 \mathrm { \AA } )
and junction lines composed of five-fold twin
structure and surface atoms
( \mathrm { h } _ { \mathrm { c } } { = } 3 4 6 \mathrm { \AA } )
, are shown on the right side of
Fig. 5(c). It suggests not only QLs but also other kinds of junction lines
play an important role in this process.
3.5. The dynamic characteristics of Surface-initiated destabilization and
its atomistic mechanism
In this section, we first aim to quantitatively describe this surface-
initiated destabilization for (001) surfaces, i.e., locate the interfaces
between the Schwarz crystal and the single crystals, which is realized
based on a potential-energy-based method. Once the interface positions
at different times are obtained, the speed of destabilization can be
determined. To be specific, this method employs time-resolved layer-
wise potential energy profiles, as shown in Fig. 6(a). In this figure,
vertically resolved atomic potential energy exhibits initial periodicity
(at
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t } = 0
ps, represented by the light blue profile) with an amplitude of 30
meV/atom, which is equivalent to a thermal differential of approxi-
mately
3 5 0 ~ \mathrm { K }
arising from periodic microstructural features. Particu-
larly, the elevated potential energy inherent to QLs domains creates
local thermodynamic fluctuation that initiate defect nucleation prior to
bulk structural collapse.
Progressive destabilization dynamics are quantitatively captured
through time-resolved potential-energy evolution analysis. At
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t } = 3 7 5
ps,
surface-proximal regions
( < 8 0 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }
from interfaces) exhibit thermody-
namic stabilization with energy remaining relatively constant, while
bulk zones
( > 8 0 \mathrm { \AA } )
display periodic oscillation characteristics, as evi-
denced in Fig. 6(a). This energy profile provides compelling evidence of
surface-mediated crystalline degradation propagating inward, with the
interface clearly demarcated between regions of stable low potential
energy and those of highly fluctuating potential energy. At
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t = } 4 5 0
ps, the
near-complete transformation from Schwarz structure to single crystal
configuration produces a vertically uniform potential energy distribu-
tion with periodic oscillation characteristics confined to a small spatial
domain. (Fig. 6(a)).
According to the aforementioned criterion, the positions of the in-
terfaces at different times during the destabilization process can be
quantitatively obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 6(b), where grey arrows
represent the locations of upper and lower interfaces. It is observed that
the upper and lower interfaces roughly move at an average speed of 82
\mathbf { m } / s
(Fig. S16), which is slower than the speed of sound but faster than
the typical rate of grain growth [55,56]. Since the interface migration
here primarily via dislocation slide (elaborated in Fig. 8), the speed of
interface migration should be comparable to the velocity of dislocation
motion. However, in the absence of shear stress and with only
temperature-activated dislocation motion, the velocity of dislocation
motion is significantly lower than its limit [57] (i.e. the transverse wave
speed in copper, which is
2 . 3 \mathrm { k m } / s
In fact, the destabilization process is non-uniform, which is reflected
by the evolutions of QLs and grains number (seen in the upper part of
Fig. 7). The initial perfect model, which consists of a high-density
population of QLs and is visualized in the bottom-left of Fig. 7 (with
HCP atoms in red and other atoms in grey), undergoes surface melting
(see Fig. 4(d)), as evidenced by the abrupt reduction in the number of
QLs and grains within the first 37.5 ps. Subsequently, the disordered
surface layer undergoes reconstruction. During this process (from 37.5
to 287.5 ps), the number of QLs remains constant, but there is a slight
reduction in the number of grains. Compared to the initial intact
structure, surface QLs are destroyed in this stage (as observed at
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t } = 2 5 0
ps). After that, the decrease in the number of QLs and grains accelerates
and is associated with detwinning in the vicinity of QLs
\scriptstyle ( \mathrm { t } = 3 0 0
ps,
corresponding details are in Fig. 8). Each annihilation event of QLs
precipitates an accelerated collapse of GBs, manifesting as multiple
plateaus in the temporal evolution of the QL number (see Fig. 7). As time
further increases to 412.5 ps, internal QLs begin to degrade. When
\scriptstyle \mathbf { t } = 5 0 0
ps, the number of QLs equals to zero, indicating that Schwarz
crystal with (001) surfaces has almost degraded, with only a few
disordered atoms and stacking faults remaining.
Throughout the entire process, destabilization takes place from
surfaces to the interior region (as illustrated in Fig. 7 and Fig. S17), the
number of QLs and grains gradually decreases over time, and ultimately

rection at different times. (b) The snapshot of potential energy distribution, where atoms are colored according to their potential energy. The high-energy and low-

reduces to zero, indicating a correlation between them.
The microscopic mechanism of destabilization is closely tied to the
degradation of QLs structures, and this relationship should be elabo-
rated. The “initiating event” for destabilization indicates the destruction
of QLs near the surface layer at high temperatures, after which det-
winning will occur (Fig. 8). Fig. 8 illustrates the degradation of QLs
during the destabilization process, with insets providing a closer view of
these structures. Within just tens of picoseconds, QLs undergo thermal-
induced breakdown. This breakdown is facilitated by the gradual
disappearance of surrounding CTBs atoms, a process known as detwin-
ning, which occurs through the gliding of
Shockley partial
dislocations (highlighted by green lines). This detwinning mechanism is
well-documented in the previous literature [59–61]. Notably, the det-
winning process progresses sequentially from the outer layers towards
the interior, ultimately leading to the elimination of QLs. These QLs
function as anchor point, stabilizing the GB network and playing a
crucial role in maintaining thermal stability.
3.6. Band-shaped destabilization: the gerneal surface-initiated
destablization mode in Schwarz crystal
In this section, we aim to uncover the general band-shaped desta-
bilization mode in Schwarz crystals. Two representative surfaces, i.e.
(111) and (110), are chosen to study this destabilization mode, that are
achieved through rotations applied to (001) surfaces and are shown in
Fig. 9(a-b). Taking the (111) surface as an example, the blue line de-
lineates (111) surface, with red and grey representing CTBs and curved
GBs, respectively (Fig. 9(a)). The QLs are highlighted with yellow cir-
cles, and the orientation along with the direction from themselves to its
nearest-neighbor QLs (indicated by black arrows) forms a
angle
with the surface. Similarly, the corresponding angle for (110) surface is
9 0 ^ { \circ }
(Fig. 9(b)). Besides, the distances between adjacent QLs are dis-
played in Fig. 9(a-b). The higher potential energy of the QL renders it as
preferential activation sites for GB degradation at elevated tempera-
tures, inducing band-shaped destabilization along a specific direction, as
illustrated by grey blocks (Fig. 9(a-b)).
To validate this mechanism, high-temperature relaxation simula-
tions are performed on Schwarz crystals with (111) or (110) surfaces.
Taking the (111) surface as an example, after relaxation at 1200 K for
1250 ps, a band-shaped destabilization zone (denoted by grey atoms)
emerges within the GB network along the predicted direction (i.e. the
orientation is
), while other areas remain unaffected (Fig. 9(c)).
Subsequently, the destabilization zone undergoes local reconstruction,
accompanied by destabilization in the adjacent region. For the (110)
surface, the band-shaped destabilization is also observed after high-
temperature relaxation (Fig. 9(d)), and the orientation is
9 0 ^ { \circ }
, which is
also consistent with the previous analysis. In reality, band-shaped
destabilization typically exhibits a lack of sensitivity to the precise
atomic arrangement present at the surface (Fig. S18-S19).
Consistent with theoretical prediction, the band-shaped thermal
destabilization patterns observed in Schwarz crystals with (111) and
(110) surfaces are inherently dictated by the spatial distribution of QLs.
Specifically, the orientation of these destabilization bands point from QL
to its nearest-neighbor.

According to the above analysis, the orientation of band-shaped
destabilization can be predicted theoretically. Since QLs are relatively
weak points, and it becomes evident that QLs serve as preferential
activation sites for structure destabilization. Hence, the surface desta-
bilization orientations of the Schwarz crystal are determined by the
nearest neighbor orientation of QLs (Fig. 9(a-b)). The angle between the
orientation of band-shaped destabilization and the normal direction of
surface can be written as
where
V _ { n }
is the normal direction of the surface,
denotes the unit
vector oriented along the [110] direction, which is utilized to describe
the nearest neighbor orientation of QLs.
Notably, the instability temperatures of Schwarz crystals with (111)
and (110) surfaces are demonstrably elevated compared to those with
(001) surfaces (Fig. S20). This discrepancy originates from the band-
shaped destabilization propagating into the bulk region of Schwarz
crystals with (111) and (110) surfaces, requiring higher activation en-
ergy for initiating destabilization compared to the surface layer desta-
bilization mode in (001) surface. In fact, for the (001) surfaces, the
destabilization mode can be considered a special form of band-shaped
destabilization, with its destabilization angle equal to
0 ^ { \circ }
3.7. Surface Schwarzation in pratical application
Surface Schwarzation will be an advanced surface nanotechnology
that not only enhances the thermal stability but also improves me-
chanical properties of materials. Although the content of Schwarz
crystals in the experimental samples is relatively limited, advancements
in fabrication techniques are expected to significantly increase their
content, enabling the formation of a nanostructured layer composed of
Schwarz crystals on the material surface, while maintaining a ductile
coarse-grained interior. This novel structure may simultaneously endow
material with superior strength-ductility synergy and high surface
thermal stability.
The surface orientation of Schwarz crystals can be tailored to sup-
press premature surface destabilization, thereby enhancing surface
thermal stability. Based on our findings, Schwarz crystals with different
surface orientations exhibit varying degrees of thermal stability. This
implies that for microstructural design and practical applications, opti-
mizing surface orientation is of critical importance.
Furthermore, Schwarz crystals may possess significant potential for
applications in corrosion and wear resistance owing to their unique GB
architecture, due to suppressed atomic diffusion and high strength [9,
10,16]. However, these require further systematic investigation to verify
its feasibility.
To synthesize thermally stable nanostructures with Schwarz crystal
configurations, severe plastic deformation (SPD) at low temperature
serves as an indispensable and critical processing step, as demonstrated
in previous research results [9,13,14,16,17]. However, the fraction of
the Schwarz crystal achieved is limit. To surmount this limitation (i.e.
further increase the fraction of Schwarz crystal), additional annealing
for grain boundary relaxation may offer a feasible route. Such annealing
would also facilitates the elimination of defects (e.g., dislocations and
J. Lin et al.
Acta Materialia 303 (2026) 121730
Decomposition of QLs
Fig. 8. The destabilization mechanism of QLs. Evolution of QLs decomposition, where red and grey atoms represent HCP and other atoms, respectively, and green
lines denote the
Shockley partial dislocations. The local enlarged diagrams are shown in the upper region.
vacancies) introduced during prior plastic deformation. Our studies
highlight the critical role of surface thermal stability in forming ther-
mally stable Schwarz crystal nanostructures as surface grain structure
destabilization can trigger the subsequent breakdown of the grain
structure in the bulk region. Therefore, selecting a moderate annealing
temperature is essential to preserve the stability of the Schwarz crystal.
The annealing temperature should lie between the transition tempera-
ture (from the Kelvin crystal to the Schwarz crystal) and the critical
instability temperature of Schwarz crystals with surfaces. For example,
800 K appears to be a suitable temperature for Schwarz crystal Cu
having a grain size of approximately
4 . 4 5 \ \mathrm { n m }
, at which its surfaces
remain stable while GBs undergo relaxation.
4. Conclusion
In this paper, the thermal stability of Schwarz crystals with surfaces
is systematically studied. The key findings can be summarized as
follows:
1. Schwarz crystals are capable of preserving their structural integrity
at relatively high temperatures, even when their surfaces are
exposed. This characteristic endows them with significant potential
for high-temperature applications and foundation for surface
Schwarzation.
2. Before destabilization, (001) surface GB reconstruction phenomenon
is discovered, during this process QLs formation takes place near the
surfaces mainly including stable QLs analogous to those in the bulk.
This characteristic structure helps stabilize the surface at high
temperatures.
3. The thermal destabilization of Schwarz crystal with (001) surfaces is
initiated at the surface, and progresses gradually from the surfaces to
the interior at an average speed of
8 2 \mathrm { m } / s
, involving the destruction
of QLs and subsequent detwinning.
4. The correlation between instability temperature and surface energy
is uncovered for (001) surfaces, i.e., the higher the surface energy,
the lower the critical instability temperature.
5. For surface orientations deviating from (001), a more ubiquitous
phenomenon of thermally induced band-shaped destabilization is
discovered, with its orientation being governed by the QLs’
distribution.
Our work reveals the unique forms and mechanisms underlying
surface GB reconstruction and its initiating destabilization in Schwarz
crystals, enhancing the fundamental understanding of their exceptional
surface thermal stability, and laying the theoretical groundwork for the
application of surface Schwarzation.
